text.title: Trello Automation and API Rules
text.summary: Butler automation patterns, REST API integration rules, and agent-usage gotchas for Trello.

rule.statement: Use Butler rules for mechanical state transitions: move card when all checklists are complete, add "Blocked" label and notify on manual block, post stale-card comment after 7 days of inactivity. Document all Butler rules in constitution.md — agents cannot create or modify Butler rules via API.
rule.rationale: Butler rules are UI-configured only; no API endpoint exists. Agents must treat Butler automation as pre-existing infrastructure.

example.note: Butler rule: when all checklists on a card are complete → move to Done. Butler rule: when card has no activity for 7 days AND is not in Done → add "Stale" label and post comment. These are configured once in the board UI and documented in constitution.md.

rule.statement: All write operations require board/list/card IDs (not names). Agents must do a read pass first to map human-readable names to IDs. Store board ID, list IDs, and label IDs in .aidocs/memory/ or constitution.md — do not fetch them on every session.
rule.rationale: Trello API requires numeric/hash IDs for all writes. Agents that skip the read pass fail silently or operate on wrong lists.

rule.statement: Respect the rate limit: 100 requests per 10 seconds per token. Parallel agent operations that create or move multiple cards simultaneously will hit this limit. Batch sequentially with a small delay or use n8n for bulk operations.
rule.rationale: Rate limit violations return 429 errors that agents must handle; unhandled, they cause silent data loss.

rule.statement: Moving a card to Done via API does not trigger Butler card-archive automation. If archiving is required, make a separate API call: PUT /cards/{id} with closed=true.
rule.rationale: Butler automation fires on UI events, not on API-triggered state changes. Agents that rely on Butler archive automation after an API move will leave cards unarchived.

antipattern.description: Creating a card with idList set by fetching the list name on every API call.
antipattern.reason: Wastes 1 API call per card write. Store list IDs in session memory after the first fetch.

rule.statement: Store TRELLO_KEY and TRELLO_TOKEN in environment variables or 1Password. Never hardcode in agent prompts, scripts, or configuration files.
rule.rationale: Trello tokens have board-level write access. Exposed tokens allow full board modification by anyone who reads the file.
